nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, lagoons, and reef slopes; commonly found on dead coral rubble, hard substrate, and mixed-sediment zones

decorPreferences: open placement away from other corals
avoid placement near corals you don't want overgrown

diet.notes: Fully photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae; does not require target feeding. The distinctive pulsing motion of the polyps appears to aid CO2 uptake for photosynthesis, substantially boosting photosynthetic efficiency. No supplemental feeding of plankton or nutrients is generally needed.

goodWith: other soft corals with adequate spacing
LPS corals if kept at a distance
avoidWith: adjacent corals of any kind without containment barriers
SPS corals in close proximity — Xenia spreading can smother them
other Xeniidae species if tank space is limited

summary: Xenia is a genus of soft corals known for the rhythmic, wave-like pulsing of their polyps, which aids photosynthesis. Generally considered one of the easiest corals to keep, Xenia is also notoriously unpredictable — established colonies can crash suddenly or spread to dominate a tank.
careNotes:
Xenia is a genus of roughly 54 described species sold under a variety of trade names, including Pulsing Xenia, Pumping Xenia, and Waving Hand Coral. The most common aquarium representative is X. umbellata and closely related pulsing types. A species formerly sold as X. elongata has since been reclassified as Unomia stolonifera and placed in a separate genus, though the two share near-identical care requirements in the hobby.

Xenia has a complicated reputation in reef keeping. Many experienced aquarists regard it as simultaneously the easiest soft coral and one of the most frustrating to manage long-term.

Pulsing behavior: The distinctive rhythmic pulsing of the polyps is driven by photosynthetic need. Research has shown that this motion draws CO2-rich water toward the symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) living inside the coral's tissues, enhancing photosynthesis by an order of magnitude compared to resting. Pulsing may slow or stop completely if pH drops below approximately 8.3, under low-intensity lighting, or with excessively strong or laminar (straight-line) flow. Cessation of pulsing is not necessarily a sign of imminent decline — colonies have been documented continuing to reproduce even while not pulsing.

Unpredictability: Xenia is well-documented as frustratingly boom-or-bust. Established colonies have been known to crash suddenly and completely without any apparent trigger or change in water parameters. Conversely, a fragment placed in a compatible tank tends to spread rapidly. This pattern is poorly understood and widely discussed among hobbyists.

Spreading: Under favorable conditions, Xenia tends to spread by sending runners (thin connecting tissue called stolons) along rockwork, glass, and substrate, potentially overgrowing neighboring corals within weeks to months. In documented invasive contexts, related Xeniidae species have been observed covering up to 80% of available benthic (seafloor) habitat in affected reef zones. In the aquarium, periodic pruning with scissors is commonly needed. Placing Xenia on isolated frag plugs, islands, or easily-removable rubble allows controlled removal. It is generally not recommended as a first coral addition to a tank housing prized SPS (small-polyp stony) corals.

Flow: Low-to-moderate, non-laminar (random or tumbling) flow is generally preferred. Excessive direct flow tends to suppress pulsing and can stress the colony. A single small powerhead or gentle gyre-type flow is typically adequate.

Lighting: Xenia tends to tolerate a wide range of intensities (approximately 50–150 PAR). Growth rate tends to increase under brighter light, but the coral can generally thrive under modest reef lighting. No particular spectrum preference has been documented.

Water quality: Xenia is generally considered more tolerant of imperfect water conditions than SPS corals, but pH stability in the range of 8.1–8.3 appears important for maintaining pulsing behavior. No iodine supplementation is required.

Reef compatibility: Xenia is generally considered reef safe in that it does not sting neighboring corals chemically. However, its spreading behavior can smother slower-growing neighbors through physical encroachment, and its tendency to encrust rockwork and substrate is the primary compatibility concern rather than aggression in the traditional sense.

Color morphs: Varieties marketed as 'Silver Xenia,' 'Blue Xenia,' 'Pom-Pom Xenia,' and others are color morphs within the same genus. A 'True Blue Xenia' that does not pulse but displays striking blue coloration has also been documented in the hobby.
breedingNotes: Xenia reproduces rapidly in captivity both asexually (by fragmentation and runner/stolon extension) and sexually. Fragging is simple: cut a polyp cluster with scissors or a blade and attach to rubble or a frag plug with reef-safe gel. Colonies can detach naturally and reattach elsewhere in the tank. Fragged colonies recover quickly under good conditions. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not deliberately practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Soft Corals & Polyps (category, Xenia not currently listed separately)
primary.notes: LiveAquaria's Xenia product page was not accessible during research (no dedicated species page found in catalog at time of access). Care parameters sourced from secondary sources; LiveAquaria used as primary source reference per slice definition.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Why Did My Pulse Corals Stop Pulsing?
additional.notes: Key husbandry source: pH threshold for pulsing (8.3), flow preferences (low-moderate, non-laminar), photosynthetic nutrition, colony crash behavior, 23-year captive colony notes. Cross-verified water parameters and flow.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Pulsing Xenia Mystery Solved
additional.notes: Scientific research on pulsing mechanism (CO2/RuBisCo photosynthesis aid). Confirms photosynthetic, no plankton feeding needed. Oxygen concentration affects pulsing cessation.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Unomia is the New Name of Xenia elongata
additional.notes: Taxonomic note: X. elongata reclassified as Unomia stolonifera. Confirms extreme growth rate ('xenia reactors'). Family Xeniidae confirmed.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Xenia (genus)
additional.notes: Taxonomy: Order Malacalcyonacea, Family Xeniidae (cross-confirmed via NCBI). 54 recognized species. X. umbellata and X. elongata noted. Indo-Pacific and Red Sea range. Pulsing behavior described.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Show Soft Corals Some Love (shallow lagoon tank)
additional.notes: Practical tank observations: thrived in 200L shallow lagoon with minimal dosing, no supplemental feeding needed, requires regular pruning. Called 'Antichrist among most experienced reefers' for spreading. Confirmed low-moderate flow tolerance.
